g.data-narration: Welcome to Chained Exceptions in Java. Learn how to preserve error history across application layers.
g.text: university 2022 Scheme - Exception Handling Deep Dive

g.data-narration: Exception chaining links one exception to another, preserving the complete error history from root cause to final error.
g.text: A mechanism that links one exception to another, preserving the complete error
history from the original cause to the final exception thrown to the caller.

g.data-narration: Without chaining, low-level errors like database failures get lost in layered applications, making debugging nearly impossible.
g.text: In multi-layered apps (Servlet → Service → DAO → Database), the actual error occurs deep
within, but needs to reach upper layers meaningfully. Without chaining, root cause is LOST!

g.data-narration: Java provides two primary mechanisms: initCause() to set cause after creation, and chained constructors for cleaner code.
initCause(Throwable cause) sets the cause and returns a reference. Can only be called once - subsequent calls throw IllegalStateException.
g.text: Throwable initCause(Throwable cause) - Sets the cause, returns 'this' reference
Throwable getCause() - Returns the cause, or null if not set

g.data-narration: Chained constructors like Exception(String message, Throwable cause) are preferred - more concise and less error-prone.
g.text: Most exception classes provide constructors accepting a cause parameter:

rect.text: Exception(String message, Throwable cause) | RuntimeException(String message, Throwable cause)

g.data-narration: When an exception is caught and re-thrown, wrap the original as the cause. The full chain displays when printStackTrace() is called.
Here's a practical example: catching a low-level LowLevelException and throwing a more meaningful HighLevelException.
In file operations, wrap FileNotFoundException in IOException to provide more context while preserving the root cause.
g.text: throw new IOException("Cannot read: " + filename, e);

g.data-narration: Use getCause() in a while loop to traverse the entire exception chain until null is returned - the end of the chain.
Design custom exceptions with chaining support by providing constructors that accept a Throwable cause parameter.
g.text: ApplicationException(String msg, Throwable cause) { super(msg, cause); }
For cleaner API in your application

g.data-narration: In servlet-JSP-database architectures, chain low-level DB exceptions into high-level business exceptions at each layer.
Best practices: always chain when catching low-level exceptions, use meaningful types, include original message, avoid over-chaining.
Common mistakes: forgetting to chain, calling initCause() twice, not providing cause constructors in custom exceptions.
Remember these key methods: initCause() sets cause once, getCause() retrieves it, and printStackTrace() shows the complete chain.
Exam tips: initCause() can only be called once, chained constructors are preferred, getCause() returns null if not set.
g.text: • initCause() throws IllegalStateException if cause already set
• getCause() returns null when no cause set
• Works with both Exception and Error

g.data-narration: Chained exceptions preserve error history across layers. Use chained constructors, provide cause constructors in custom exceptions.
Thanks for learning! Practice these concepts to build robust, debuggable Java applications. All the best for your exams!
g.text: Practice chaining exceptions in your Java projects
